message:
That's the Heller decision, and was very controversial for many reasons, not the least of which was that it completely overhauled how the Supreme Court treated guns.  ANYWHO, that's irrelevant to what was said.  There has not been any case law where the declared purpose of the 2nd amendment is to allow people to fight against the government of the United States.

Further evidence that Scalia intentionally misinterpreted the 2nd amendment would be the initial drafts of the amendment and prior examples used in drafting the amendment, e.g. the Virginia state constitution which says "That a well regulated militia, composed of the body of the people, trained to arms, is the proper, natural, and safe defense of a free state, therefore, the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed; …"  If it seems familiar, it should; because that's the basis for the 2nd amendment, and clearly defines the purpose of being armed, and needless to say it's not to fight against our own elected government, no matter how tyrranical it may be/appear.

> And yet you provide absolutely no argument that human nature ISN'T egoism

Human nature is not capitalist nature. It is capitalist nature that is greed as it is encouraged by the system itself. Being selfish or sharing is a learned behavioural choice determined by the system you have grown in. Egalitarianism has existed for thousands of years, before capitalism and in some parts even during it.

This trope that "greed is human nature" is only perpetuated among those at the top and is unforunately believed by some under them. Hell, look at polls for whatever country you live in. You will find the majority of people always support social action, such as helping the poor, raising taxes, expanding healthcare, education reform, etc etc. The only reason these things do not happen is because the political systems we reside in are not democracies. 

> And then you rediscover the Economic Calculation Problem and your society needlessly suffers after failing to learn from the past.

This argument would have more merit if we were living in a pre-1970s world where technology literally could not handle calculations on such a scale. Look at any large international company today. They are all centrally planned and do more calculations than ex nations such as the USSR could ever hope for.

> fair share

Anything less than the value you produce is not fair.

> they agreed to

The bargaining power difference between an employer and employee is so vast that you can not say they fairly agreed to it.

> well why should anyone else get a say other than the people who own it or have been entrusted with it?

Why not have a better system in place in which the workers themselves operate the business, and thus you can leave out the immense power inequality. Then you'd have a [more efficient, more equal, and longer lasting business](https://www.uk.coop/sites/default/files/uploads/attachments/worker_co-op_report.pdf).

> Everyone profits of their labor

Impossible under capitalism, at least for a normal worker. And even if they did, under the relationships of capitalism, you have no say in how those profits are given out, what they are used for, or how the business operates.

message:
> The only reason these things do not happen is because the political systems we reside in are not democracies. 

That is a horrible misrepresentation of the political views of your opponent. It widdles their opinions down to below "worthless" because they can't possibly have thoughts and opinions for themselves. 

Look up the pools on how many people don't want the GOVERNMENT to do all of that. The results may suprise you. Basically, people are untrustworthy of the government given the history of governing throughout the world, and probably have different ideas about how to achieve this goals. 

You just want to use weasle words to get poeple on your side. Maybe you should stay listening to people instead of trying to reframe their worldview for them. 

> Look at any large international company today. They are all centrally planned and do more calculations than ex nations such as the USSR could ever hope for.

That's not just what it's about. The problem is the central planners are horrible at predicting demand, and have no personal interest in fulfilling it. Yes, people may want Xbox's but if the planners want to just continue with the Atari (because who really needs video games), well the people are shit out of luck. 

Wants and desires become political, and fulfilling demand becomes harder and harder as everyone tries to get society to spend resources on what they think society should want. Look at NASA vs SpaceX. Everyone's trying to get NASA to more of something (more deep space exploration, mane Mars missions, more space infrastructure projects, or more Earth Climate research, etc). Meanwhile Musk just to ok the money he owned and started on a project that would double or triple NASA's capabilities by lowering costs with reusable rockets. 

> Anything less than the value you produce is not fair.

Why!

> The bargaining power difference between an employer and employee is so vast that you can not say they fairly agreed to it.

The bargaining power reflects simple facts about the market, facts that wouldn't change with a chance of economic system. The simple fact is an individual is always has less bargaining power than a group of people. This is why I don't see anything wrong with unions, and think we should start more, not abandon what's been works decently great (especially compared to other economic systems). 

> Why not have a better system in place in which the workers themselves operate the business, and thus you can leave out the immense power inequality. 

I'm all for employee owns businesses, I just don't like the idea behind stealing from those who created productive work. 

> Impossible under capitalism,

Because your definition of profit only works to fulfill a present mindset.

"@impulsive-line, I hear your points, but you're missing the forest for the trees. It's not about reframing anyone's worldview, it's about challenging the status quo. And yes, people want Xboxes, but capitalism has shown it can deliver that. Can it deliver clean water, quality education, and healthcare for all? That's the question. As for unions, they're a bandaid, not a cure. We need systemic change, not just better bargaining power."

>Why though? At what point is it the band's responsibility to make things better with the resources they have/are given?

It's like saying. What RESPONSIBILITY do I have as a shop owner. To lower my prices? The responsibility part is irrelevant if you want to stay competitive. If you rigidly refuse to set your prices accordingly to market forces. You will go out of business.

Same here. If a state refuses to deal with very real real life events affecting a group of people. Then that will then bite them in the ass and their people. But unlike going bankrupt you don't REALLY see the result of your actions as clearly on the scales of nations. 

You don't see the alternate universe where you did invest into Native American growth and now they are responsible for billions in trade with you.

Or where you invested absolutely nothing. Now the groups being even in worse shape, being a perpetual drain on US resources.

A prime example is the humanitarian aid in Africa. It's another continent, it's far away, what CONCIEVABLE benefit it could ever provide us? Well today African nations are the fastest growing developing nations, where the growth is directly correlated with elimination of pandemics, and the creation of infrastructures (such as water supplies, hospitals, services, etc...), and are now responsible for 37 billions of trade yearly with US. That's what nearly a  triple the investment of 15 billions to African aid.

This just happens to be one of the areas where literally no matter how you look at it, it is always worth to invest.

message:
Employees also have a stake in the company doing well, since that would mean better job security and higher wages under good management. Employees, if they follow their self interest, would vote for management that is competent and would like to expand the business. 

Also, employees have a bigger stake in the success of a company than investors do. For most employees, their jobs are their only source of income, and switching careers is much more difficult than selling off assets. 

To simplify: let me ask you a question. What is a more valuable contribution: your time or your money? An employee invests their time into a company; an investor their money. Which do you believe is making a larger commitment?

This is the popular argument I keep seeing.
I disagree with this opinion on a couple of aspects:
1. It assumes dependence between people, my choice of action doesn't dictate other peoples choices or even relate as we aren't rational beings and I may hold an unpopular opinion.
2. If people "probably" chose to pick up trash then my affect is reduced to a negligible amount.
3. I generally don't benefit from picking up trash other people are already going to pick up.

Overall, in situations where it is likely that someone else will take care of the problem (excluding situations where I am actually negatively affected) I shouldn't even think about doing it if I don't stand to gain from it.

I wanted to try something different than the obvious "the lack of a vote for Hillary is not a vote for trump, it's just what it is, a non-hillary non-trump vote." That doesn't seem to convince many people to view the topic any differently even though it's correct.

I hate both options fairly equally, so the third option is to vote for Jill Stein, the candidate whose views most closely align with mine. Will she be elected? Not a chance. But rather than split hairs over which option is the best of two awful ones over the next 4 years, I'm going to contribute my vote towards Jill Stein, in the hopes that it helps the green party reach the 5% of the vote needed to receive federal funding for their next campaign. I think contributing to the possibility of a future where we don't have just two options to choose from is a much better third option than relenting and voting for someone who I'm going to hate and who I don't believe in.

Hell, we should probably question the partiality of any judge who wants to make America great (again, or for the first time), because they'd have a vested interest in ruling in his favor.  It couldn't be a conservative, because they'd have the same interest...but it couldn't be a liberal either, because they'd have an interest in ruling against him.

So if we accept his argument, who *would* be the appropriate judge?  What racial group can we trust to be dispassionate?  What gender?  What political affiliation?

Edit - the "Absolutely right" is sarcastic/rhetorical, in case that's confusing anyone.

message:
What form of libertarianism are you writing off? Milton Friedman called himself a libertarian and is widely regarded as one of the greatest economists of the 20th century. Here's him [debating](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w3-_r_t7AZU) a young David Brooks (New York Times columnist), who [later said](http://magazine.uchicago.edu/0402/features/index-brooks.shtml) Friedman's two-sentence rebuttals turned him from a socialist to a conservative.

Friedman can't be dismissed as ignorant; he's the sort of person you hold in the most highest and respectful regard among the people you disagree with.
his believes were also widly different from the average run-of-the-mill libertarian, so OPs point is still valid.
@concave-fleece, while Friedman was indeed a libertarian, his views were more nuanced than many modern libertarians. He supported some social safety nets, unlike many libertarians today.
@tossed-vault, Nuance doesn't change the fact that he was against most social programs. He's still not the poster child for libertarianism that OPs trying to make him out to be.

"@soft-entropy, @tossed-vault, Can we just agree that Milton Friedman was a complex human being and move on? Or are we all just going to keep dancing in circles like a bunch of libertarian flamingos?"

message: Keeping a Facebook profile allows me to use Facebook messenger. Facebook messenger is the messaging app of choice for may of my associates and have Facebook messenger available to me (in addition to other, better, messaging services) allows me to facilitate conversation with those groups more easily. Ergo, there is a benefit to keeping a Facebook profile. When using a Facebook profile in that way (by setting it to totally private and not posting anything ever), you minimize the drawbacks of keeping a profile while retaining the benefits previously outlined. In my situation, the benefits of keeping a Facebook account outweigh the drawbacks.
